file_name: Mahmud and Naim - 2025 - Skin cancer classification using VGG-16.json

did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a deep learning approach, specifically the VGG-16 convolutional neural network (CNN) architecture with transfer learning, to classify dermoscopic images into melanoma and non-melanoma (benign vs. malignant) categories.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a deep learning model (VGG-16) for skin cancer classification. It details specific methods including data collection (3,297 images from ISIC/Kaggle), preprocessing, transfer learning implementation, and training parameters (epochs, batch size, optimizer). It reports original results such as training accuracy (99.62%) and validation accuracy (84.97%), and compares its performance against other models, which characterizes it as an original research study rather than a review.

title: Skin Cancer Classification Using VGG-16
journal: International Journal of Innovative Science and Research Technology
brief_summary: This study developed a deep learning model using the VGG-16 convolutional neural network with transfer learning to classify dermoscopic images into melanoma and non-melanoma categories. The dataset comprised 3,297 images sourced from the ISIC dataset and Kaggle, split into 2,637 training and 660 testing samples. Preprocessing steps included resizing images to 224x224 pixels, normalization, and data augmentation techniques such as horizontal and vertical flipping. The model was trained using the Adam optimizer with a learning rate of 0.002 over 30 epochs, achieving a training accuracy of 99.62% and a validation accuracy of 84.97%. The results indicate that the VGG-16 model outperformed baseline architectures like NASNet Mobile and Inception V3, demonstrating its potential for reliable clinical support in early skin cancer diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: Used as one of the two binary classification targets. The dataset contained 1,498 malignant images (1,197 in training, 300 in testing) out of 3,297 total images.

Used as the comparator class to melanoma in a binary classification task. The dataset contained 1,800 benign images (1,440 in training, 360 in testing) out of 3,297 total images. Specific histological subtypes (e.g., nevus, BCC) were not distinguished in the study's labels.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification of dermoscopic images into melanoma (malignant) and non-melanoma (benign) categories.
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the goal is to 'classify skin cancer' and 'differentiate between benign and malignant skin lesions' using a VGG-16 model. The dataset consists of two classes: 1,498 malignant images and 1,800 benign images. The model outputs discrete diagnostic labels (melanoma vs. non-melanoma) rather than continuous values.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopic skin images sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) and made available via Kaggle. The study utilized a curated subset of this dataset for binary classification of skin lesions.

use.training: Used for training the VGG-16 model with transfer learning. Images were resized to 224x224 pixels, normalized, and augmented (horizontal/vertical flipping).
use.testing: Used for testing the model's performance. The split was 80% training and 20% testing.
use.other: The dataset was originally associated with the ISBI 2016 challenge for melanoma detection. The original dataset did not have labels, so a labeling process was applied by the authors to classify images into benign and malignant categories.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, recall, and precision metrics compared against other models.

ISIC Dataset (via Kaggle).notes: The VGG-16 model outperformed baseline architectures (NASNet Mobile, NASNet Large, Inception V3, CNN, Ensemble CNN) in terms of accuracy, recall, and precision. The model was trained for 30 epochs with a batch size of 32 and a learning rate of 0.002 using the Adam optimizer. Convolutional layers were frozen, and only fully connected layers were retrained.

architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained on the ImageNet dataset (1.2 million training images, 50,000 validation, 100,000 testing across 1,000 categories). Transfer learning was applied by freezing the convolutional layers and retraining only the fully connected layers.

architecture_details.layer_structure: 16 layers total (convolutional and fully connected). Uses small 3x3 filters with a stride of 1 pixel. Five blocks of convolutional layers, each followed by a MaxPooling layer.

hyperparameters.image_preprocessing: Resized to 224x224, normalized, mean filter for noise reduction, median filter for edge preservation

training_details.training_schedule: Convolutional layers were frozen (trainable=False) to keep weights unchanged; only fully connected layers were retrained.

preprocessing_techniques: Data augmentation via horizontal and vertical flipping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to increase the size of the dataset; specific probabilities or ranges were not reported.
Applied to increase the size of the dataset; specific probabilities or ranges were not reported.

conclusions: The proposed VGG-16 model with transfer learning effectively differentiates between benign and malignant skin lesions, achieving a training accuracy of 99.62% and a validation accuracy of 84.97%.
The VGG-16 model outperformed baseline architectures including NASNet Mobile, NASNet Large, Inception V3, conventional CNNs, and Ensemble CNN in terms of accuracy, recall, and precision.
The study highlights the potential of transfer learning with deep CNNs to improve early and accurate diagnosis of skin cancer, particularly melanoma, which is crucial for reducing mortality rates.
Future work aims to enhance classification performance by incorporating more diverse datasets and exploring hybrid ensemble models to improve generalization in real-world clinical settings.
important_remarks: The dataset labels were not originally provided; the authors applied a manual labeling process to classify the ISIC images into benign and malignant categories, introducing potential subjectivity or error in the ground truth.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on a single curated subset of the ISIC dataset split 80/20, which limits the assessment of generalizability to diverse clinical populations.

The paper does not report sensitivity, specificity, or Area Under the Curve (AUC) metrics, which are critical for evaluating diagnostic performance in medical contexts; only accuracy, recall, and precision are provided.

There is a discrepancy in reported training parameters: the abstract and results sections state 30 epochs with a batch size of 32 and learning rate of 0.002, while the Methods section describes 25 epochs with a batch size of 128 and learning rate of 0.0001.
The model achieved a very high training accuracy (99.62%) compared to validation (84.97%) and testing (86.97%) accuracy, indicating significant overfitting to the training data.

The 'benign' class is a heterogeneous group of non-melanoma lesions; the study does not distinguish between specific histological subtypes (e.g., nevus, basal cell carcinoma), limiting clinical utility for specific differential diagnoses.
Data augmentation was limited to horizontal and vertical flipping, which may be insufficient for robust feature learning in dermoscopic image analysis compared to more advanced techniques.
The study was conducted using Google Colab without reporting hardware specifications or computational cost, making reproducibility of training time difficult.
